principles.label: Think Inside the Box
principles.summary: The best marketing angle or solution lies within the intrinsic qualities of the product itself, not in external gimmicks.
principles.details: Instead of looking externally for wild ideas or relying on 'borrowed interest' like celebrity endorsements, marketers should focus deeply on the product to discover its core value and Unique Selling Proposition (USP).
principles.representative_quote: To me, the answer to the problem is intrinsic with the problem itself, it's not borrowed interest.

principles.label: Hands-On Philanthropy
principles.summary: Real philanthropic change requires rolling up your sleeves and doing the work, not just writing checks.
principles.details: Writing checks is the easy part of philanthropy. To reverse cycles of poverty, you must do deep research, understand the community, and build targeted, comprehensive solutions.
principles.representative_quote: Most philanthropists write checks... that is the easy part. The hard part is rolling up your sleeves and doing the work to make real change yourself.

principles.label: Community-Led Solutions
principles.summary: Do not enter a community with preconceived ideas; ask them directly what they need.
principles.details: Top-down solutions fail. As an outsider, you cannot accurately guess the specific, everyday fears and needs of a community. You must partner with them through focus groups and door-to-door surveys.
principles.representative_quote: We didn't just go in with a preconceived idea we went in and asked them door-to-door 40-minute interview with every household... what do you want what do you need and they told us.

principles.label: Education as the Foundation of Sustainability
principles.summary: Physical infrastructure improvements will fail without long-term investments in education and empowerment.
principles.details: Building parks and community centers is just brick and mortar. Without education and a sense of self-worth, physical improvements to a community will eventually go to ruin.
principles.representative_quote: I realized after a while that nothing was sustainable unless we started working on education.

principles.label: Capitalize on Your Gifts
principles.summary: It is a moral imperative to use the talents and opportunities you are given to their fullest extent.
principles.details: Whether channeling a creative spirit into business instead of fine arts or leveraging business success for philanthropy, failing to use your natural gifts is a failure of obligation.
principles.representative_quote: Because you have to take advantage of what you're given. If you don't, that's a sin in my mind.

principles.label: Focus Philanthropy Locally
principles.summary: Direct your philanthropic efforts to the places where your employees live and work.
principles.details: As a large employer, investing in the communities that sustain your business is the most meaningful and direct way to make an impact and 'do well by doing good.'
principles.representative_quote: I felt that if I was really going to do work I should start to do the work in the place where our employees worked and live that that would be the most meaningful.

principles.label: Authenticity is the Ultimate Currency
principles.summary: Who you work for and the values they hold are more important than the actual work you do.
principles.details: Working for someone whose values you don't share is damaging. Society values authenticity above all else, making it the most important currency in both business and personal careers.
principles.representative_quote: Who you work for and with is far more important than what you actually do.

principles.label: Give Consumers Added Value
principles.summary: Differentiate commodities by adding permanent, tangible benefits or unique packaging.
principles.details: When selling a commodity or a product with a short lifespan, adding a unique container or leaning into authentic health benefits gives consumers a specific reason to request your brand over others.
principles.representative_quote: I was desperate for a way not only to differentiate our service from ftds but to give consumers added value that would Inspire them to ask specifically for Tel floraa flowers.

frameworks.label: Test and Roll
frameworks.summary: A method for launching new direct-response products or campaigns to gauge consumer demand before mass production.
frameworks.details: 1. Create a limited run of the product or campaign. 2. Test the product in one to three regional markets. 3. Analyze the sales data and consumer response. 4. Roll out the product to a broader market based on the validated assumptions.
frameworks.representative_quote: You would test in a market, or three markets, in a limited run... And you would see how it sold, and then you would roll, so we call test and roll.

frameworks.label: Heels on the Ground Philanthropy
frameworks.summary: A comprehensive approach to revitalizing impoverished communities through direct involvement and phased intervention.
frameworks.details: 1. Conduct focus groups and in-home surveys to ask the community what they actually need. 2. Build the requested physical infrastructure to address immediate fears and needs. 3. Implement empowerment classes so the community learns to sustain itself. 4. Invest heavily in long-term education to break the cycle of poverty.
frameworks.representative_quote: It's easy to write a check but if you really get into the community or the place that you're working you really understand what the needs are and what you should do next.

frameworks.label: CareerTech Pipeline
frameworks.summary: A framework for bridging the local skills gap and fixing failing education-to-workforce pipelines.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify high school students interested in specific industry tracks. 2. Partner with local high schools and community colleges to integrate curriculum. 3. Enable students to graduate high school with a two-year Associate's degree. 4. Provide direct pathways into middle management roles at your company or onto a four-year university.
frameworks.representative_quote: When they finish high school they will have two years they will have an aa degree and they can either go into middle management at one of our companies or they can go on to a four-year school.

mental_models.label: The Unique Selling Proposition (USP) Lens
mental_models.summary: Evaluating any product or brand by identifying the singular, uncopiable trait that makes it superior, found intrinsically within the product.
mental_models.details: Used to transform commodities into premium brands by focusing on factual, inherent qualities (e.g., Fiji Water's volcanic filtration) rather than manufactured narratives.
mental_models.representative_quote: I had trained myself through my life through success and failure that the only products that work is if they're unique.

mental_models.label: The Omelas Dilemma
mental_models.summary: A moral lens for recognizing hidden suffering and evaluating one's responsibility to society.
mental_models.details: Based on a story about a perfect town whose happiness relies on the hidden torture of a single child. It represents the idea that one cannot ethically enjoy a perfect life of privilege while ignoring the suffering of the workers or people who help make that life possible.
mental_models.representative_quote: I realized that that is my obligation as a human being on this planet to give back as much as I can.

mental_models.label: Commodity to Premium
mental_models.summary: Viewing basic agricultural or natural commodities as potential luxury goods by layering on unique packaging, health research, and high-end brand positioning.
mental_models.details: Taking standard items like water, pomegranates, or mandarins and elevating them through distinctive, hard-to-manufacture packaging and authentic health narratives.

mental_models.label: Companies are like people
mental_models.summary: Viewing businesses as having biological life cycles to determine how much executive intervention they require.
mental_models.details: A company in its 'toddler stage' requires different leadership and time allocation than a 'mature' company that might need reinvention.
mental_models.representative_quote: Companies are like people. They have a life cycle...

heuristics.label: Think inside the box
heuristics.summary: Look inside the product itself to find its Unique Selling Proposition rather than looking externally for marketing gimmicks.
heuristics.representative_quote: To me, the answer to the problem is intrinsic with the problem itself, it's not borrowed interest.

heuristics.label: Ask door-to-door
heuristics.summary: Ask a community what they need instead of guessing from the outside.
heuristics.representative_quote: We didn't just go in with a preconceived idea we went in and asked them door-to-door.

heuristics.label: The Three-Hour Hiring Rule
heuristics.summary: You will learn more about a new hire in their first three hours on the job than in six months of interviews.
heuristics.representative_quote: ...you can spend six months interviewing somebody and you'll know more the first three hours they're at work than you will ever know from those interviews.

heuristics.label: The 7 P.M. Email Cutoff
heuristics.summary: Stop answering emails after 7:00 P.M. to maintain strict boundaries with digital communication.
heuristics.representative_quote: As they come in but not after 7:00 pm.

heuristics.label: Hire better people than you
heuristics.summary: Always aim to hire staff who are more talented or capable than you are to ensure business growth.
heuristics.representative_quote: And eventually, I could hire staff and I tried to get better people than me.

signature_quotes.label: Intrinsic Problem Solving
signature_quotes.summary: On finding the marketing solution within the product itself.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: To me, the answer to the problem is intrinsic with the problem itself, it's not borrowed interest.

signature_quotes.label: The Hard Work of Philanthropy
signature_quotes.summary: On the necessity of personal involvement in charitable giving.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Most philanthropists write checks... that is the easy part. The hard part is rolling up your sleeves and doing the work to make real change yourself.

signature_quotes.label: The Sin of Wasted Talent
signature_quotes.summary: On the moral obligation to use one's natural gifts.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Because you have to take advantage of what you're given. If you don't, that's a sin in my mind.

signature_quotes.label: Asking the Community
signature_quotes.summary: On her approach to community intervention.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: We didn't just go in with a preconceived idea we went in and asked them door-to-door 40-minute interview with every household... what do you want what do you need and they told us.

signature_quotes.label: Education as Sustainability
signature_quotes.summary: On why physical infrastructure isn't enough to save a community.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I realized after a while that nothing was sustainable unless we started working on education.

signature_quotes.label: Authenticity in Work
signature_quotes.summary: On choosing who to work for.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Who you work for and with is far more important than what you actually do.

signature_quotes.label: God is in the Details
signature_quotes.representative_quote: God is in the details. I'm It's awful. I can't help it. Everything has the same importance.

anti_patterns.label: Checkbook Philanthropy
anti_patterns.summary: Simply writing checks to charities without personal involvement or oversight.
anti_patterns.details: It avoids the hard work necessary to enact real change and often fails to address the actual, nuanced needs of the community.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: It's easy to write a check but if you really get into the community or the place that you're working you really understand what the needs are and what you should do next rather than throwing money at something.

anti_patterns.label: Borrowed Interest in Marketing
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on unrelated elements like celebrity endorsements to sell a product.
anti_patterns.details: It distracts from the product's actual value and substitutes genuine intrinsic value with an unrelated external narrative.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: To me, the answer to the problem is intrinsic with the problem itself, it's not borrowed interest.

anti_patterns.label: Preconceived Community Solutions
anti_patterns.summary: Going into communities with preconceived ideas of what they need instead of asking them.
anti_patterns.details: Outsiders cannot accurately guess the specific, everyday fears and needs of a community. You end up building things people might not actually want or use.

anti_patterns.label: Diluting Premium Products
anti_patterns.summary: Watering down a premium or naturally healthy product to fit standard grocery categories.
anti_patterns.details: It strips the product of its unique value proposition and authenticity, forcing it to compete as a generic commodity rather than a premium health product.

anti_patterns.label: Over-relying on Interviews
anti_patterns.summary: Trusting the interview process too much when evaluating a candidate's fit and capabilities.
anti_patterns.details: Interviews do not reveal how a person actually works; you learn significantly more by observing them in their first few hours on the job.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: ...you can spend six months interviewing somebody and you'll know more the first three hours they're at work than you will ever know from those interviews.

anti_patterns.label: Always-On Digital Availability
anti_patterns.summary: Sleeping with your phone, giving out your personal number, or answering emails late at night.
anti_patterns.details: It removes personal boundaries, invites constant interruption, and prevents you from prioritizing family during free moments.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: No and I never give out my number.
